scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle writes from the perspective of 4th-c. Athenian social hierarchies where male authority over females was the legal and social norm.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Aristotle's floruit (-348) during the composition of the Politics, reflecting contemporary Athenian patriarchal structures.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The distinction between citizens and those fit only for 'necessary service' (slaves) is a foundational principle of the Athenian polis where Aristotle lived.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated by Aristotle at his floruit (-348); the exclusion of slaves from citizenship was a core contemporary practice of his time.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle reflects common Greek cultural attitudes regarding the 'natural' status of non-Greeks (barbarians) relative to the Greek citizen body.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects contemporary 4th-c. BCE Greek ideology regarding the ethnic dimensions of slavery during Aristotle's life.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The use of stewards (epitropoi) to manage estates while owners pursued 'politics or philosophy' was a standard practice for the Athenian upper class.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to -348, describing the contemporary leisure-based lifestyle Aristotle observed among the wealthy in his own society.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Justification of warfare for slave acquisition reflects the contemporary Greek legal and moral framework in which Aristotle operated.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated in the Politics (-348) as a justification for existing methods of labor acquisition used by contemporary Greek states.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle contrasts his own society's monetary economy with the barter systems of 'barbarians,' positioning Athens as the civilizational standard.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Written at Aristotle's floruit (-348) to explain the evolution of trade relative to contemporary 4th-c. barbarian groups.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The permanent subjugation of women was a key feature of the Athenian oikos and the broader social structure of Aristotle's era.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to -348; Aristotle frames this continuous rule as a natural and active fact of his contemporary environment.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle uses the Athenian household (oikos) as the model for describing the monarchical rule over children and republican rule over wives.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the domestic legal hierarchy of 4th-c. Athens contemporary to the author's writing (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The social stigma against manual labor and its exclusion from 'liberal' pursuits was a defining characteristic of the Athenian elite class.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the 4th-c. BCE educational and social prejudices held by the Athenian upper class during Aristotle's time.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle discusses revenue-raising tactics used by contemporary Greek poleis, including state monopolies.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Describes active economic practices and 'tricks' used by states in the 4th-c. BCE Mediterranean world.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The total exclusion of slaves from deliberative processes was a practical reality of Athenian law and political theory.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated as a contemporary psychological and legal justification for slavery during Aristotle's floruit (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The subordinate nature of female deliberation ('without authority') reflects the legal status of women as perpetual minors in Athenian law.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to -348; this rule describes the contemporary status of women in the Greek polis.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The legal dependence of children on the father (kyrios) was a fundamental pillar of Athenian family law in the 4th-c. BCE.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects contemporary Athenian laws regarding paternal authority and child status during Aristotle's life.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Rotational office-holding was the hallmark of Athenian democracy and other contemporary Greek 'republican' systems.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle describes this as the method by which contemporary states ('it does actually come about') manage power among equals.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The definition of the city as a community of citizens sharing a common site is central to Aristotle's contemporary political theory.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the standard 4th-c. BCE definition of a polis and its territorial rights.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle is critiquing the specific constitution proposed by his contemporary, Plato; this polity is the theoretical Kallipolis.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle is analyzing a contemporary rival political theory active in 4th-c. Athenian debate.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Religious pollution (miasma) and expiation based on kinship were active parts of the legal and sacral life of 4th-c. Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Describes active religious customs and legal requirements regarding family ties in Aristotle's time (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle explicitly names Sparta as the polity where the custom of sharing possessions like dogs and horses is practiced.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle describes Sparta's customs as they existed and were observed during his own lifetime (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle is specifically discussing the laws and social regulations of the Cretan poleis regarding their slave population.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle describes Cretan laws as a contemporary example of political organization in the 4th-c. BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The exclusion of Cretan slaves from bearing arms is cited as a specific regional legal feature of that polity.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Refers to the contemporary status of slaves in Crete during the 4th-c. BCE.
